title: Monitor observability via push-write trace stream (intentional divergence from pi-mono pull model)
context: Pi-mono SessionManager is pull-only: agents and extensions read session state via getBranch / getEntry / getEntries / getChildren but cannot write directly. SessionManager enforces readonly via the ReadonlySessionManager interface delivered to extensions (node_modules/@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/session-manager.d.ts). Monitors are side-channel executors — classify calls fire on turn_end and message_end events outside the main agent loop — and require independent write capability to record execution traces. Issue-023 names the capture requirement. The architectural question is whether to align with pi-mono's pull/replay observability model (structurally impossible given ReadonlySessionManager) or to diverge to a push-observability surface scoped to monitors. The 2026-04-26 mandate-bound canonical-compliance audit verified that pi-mono provides zero push-observability primitives (no Tracer, no Telemetry, no Logger framework, only event subscription) and that the divergence is the only path that satisfies the capture requirement without violating ReadonlySessionManager.
decision: Monitors implement a separate push-observable trace subsystem (TraceWriter at the pi-jit-agents executeAgent boundary) that does NOT attempt to write to the session. This is an intentional divergence from pi-mono's pull-observable architecture. The divergence is justified by three forces: monitors are not agents; classify events should not pollute session history; ReadonlySessionManager structurally prevents the pull-aligned alternative. The trace subsystem is scoped to pi-behavior-monitors and pi-jit-agents and does not generalize to other extensions. Future upstream pi-mono push-observable surfaces (if added) do not force refactor — the separation is intentional and stable.
consequences: Pi-mono remains the system of record for agent conversation and tool execution (session pull-model)
Monitors are the system of record for classification events (trace push-model)
Consumers of monitor health and classification patterns query the trace directory, not the session
Trace writes pass through schema validation extending pi-project's schema-validator pattern — net-new write-time validation discipline because pi-mono SessionManager has no equivalent

Trace data may include credentials echoed by users in prompts; pi-mono has no redaction primitives so the trace subsystem ships a comprehensive redaction layer covering all known credential shapes plus a project-extension mechanism for domain-sensitive data

Bounded growth via date-rotation (.workflows/monitors/<name>/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl), 30-day rolling retention, 500MB-per-day overflow split — mirrors pi-coding-agent's timestamp-anchored session-file naming
Configuration via PI_AGENT_TRACE_DIR, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILTER, PI_AGENT_TRACE_RETENTION_DAYS, PI_AGENT_TRACE_FILE_MAX_SIZE_MB env vars adopting pi-mono's canonical PI_* prefix convention
CLI flags --trace, --no-trace, --trace-dir <dir>, --trace-filter <file> mirroring pi-coding-agent's boolean / negation / value-arg patterns

references.label: agent_end extension hook canonical capture point
